draft.introduction: The Dream of the Rood (c. 8th–10th century) is the supreme achievement of Old English religious poetry. The Cross itself speaks, narrating the Crucifixion as a loyal retainer forced to participate in its lord’s execution—a stunning fusion of Germanic heroic values and Christian theology.

translation.original_ref: Dream of the Rood, Vercelli Book
translation.text: I dared not bow or break against the Lord’s word, though I saw the surface of the earth tremble. I could have felled all the foes, yet I stood firm. The young hero stripped himself—that was God almighty—strong and resolute. He climbed on the high gallows, bold in the sight of many, when he wished to redeem mankind.
translation.original_text: Ne dorste ic hwæðre būgan tō eorðan, feallan tō foldan scēatum, ac ic sceolde fæste standan. Rōd wæs ic ārǣred. Āhōf ic rīcne Cyning, heofona Hlāford; hyldan mē ne dorste.

translator_notes.note: Christ is described as a “young hero” who “strips himself” and “climbs” the cross with the boldness of a Germanic warrior. This is not passive suffering but active, heroic self-sacrifice—the Crucifixion recast in the language of the comitatus. The Cross, as Christ’s loyal thane, endures the agony of assisting in its lord’s death.
